content.problemStatement:
You are given a binary string S=S1S2…SNS = S_1S_2 \ldots S_NS=S1​S2​…SN​ of length NNN. You are also given an integer KKK. You can do the following operation at most N−K+1N-K+1N−K+1 times:


First, choose a substring SiSi+1…Si+K−1S_iS_{i+1} \ldots S_{i+K-1}Si​Si+1​…Si+K−1​ of SSS of length exactly KKK. This substring shouldn't have been chosen in any of the previous operations. That is, the same starting index of the substring can't be chosen twice.
Then, flip all the characters of the chosen substring (i.e. change every 000 to 111 and vice versa).
Find the lexicographically smallest string possible after the operations if you do them in an optimal way.

**Note**:


A substring is consecutive elements of a string. For eg. in the string "01011", "101" is a substring, but "111" is not a substring.
A string AAA is lexicographically smaller than string BBB, if Ai<BiA_i < B_iAi​<Bi​, where iii is the first index where AAA and BBB differ.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers: NNN, KKK.
The second line of each test case contains the string SSS.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the lexicographically smallest string that you can achieve.
constraints: Each element of SSS is either 000 or 111
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 3×1053 \times 10^53×105.
**Subtask 2 (20 points):** The sum of NNN across all test cases won't exceed 202020.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Choose the substring 1011\textcolor{red}{01}101 and flip it. So now SSS becomes 110110110. Then choose the substring 110\textcolor{red}{11}0110 and flip it. So now SSS becomes 000000000. This is the lexicographically smallest string that can be achieved, and hence this is the answer.
Test case 222: Choose the substring 10111\textcolor{red}{01}11011 and flip it. So now SSS becomes 110111011101. Then choose the substring 1101\textcolor{red}{11}011101 and flip it. So now SSS becomes 000100010001. This is the lexicographically smallest string that can be achieved, and hence this is the answer.
Test case 333: Choose the substring 010100\textcolor{red}{101}001010 and flip it. So now SSS becomes 001000010000100. Then choose the substring 0010000\textcolor{red}{100}00100 and flip it. So now SSS becomes 000110001100011. This is the lexicographically smallest string that can be achieved, and hence this is the answer.
Test case 444: Choose the substring 0101001\textcolor{red}{01}001010 and flip it. So now SSS becomes 011000110001100. Then choose the substring 011000\textcolor{red}{11}0001100 and flip it. So now SSS becomes 000000000000000. This is the lexicographically smallest string that can be achieved, and hence this is the answer.
